overview.scope: Task 1  When visiting the web service using the IP address, what is the domain that we are being redirected to? Task 2  Which scripting language is being used on the server to generate webpages? Task 3  What is the name of the URL parameter which is used to load different language versions of the webpage? Task 4  Which of the following values for the page parameter would be an example of exploiting a Local File Include (LFI) vulnerability: "french.html", "//10.10.14.6/somefile", "../../../../../../../../windows/system32/drivers/etc/hosts", "mimikatz.exe" Task 5  Which of the following values for the page parameter would be an example of exploiting a Remote File Include (RFI) vulnerability: "french.html", "//10.10.14.6/somefile", "./../../../../../../../windows/system32/drivers/etc/hosts", "mimikatz.exe" Task 6  What does NTLM stand for? Task 7  Which flag do we use in the Responder utility to specify the network interface? Task 8  There are several tools that take a NetNTLMv2 challenge/response and try millions of passwords to see if any of them generate the same response. One such tool is often referred to as john, but the full name is what?. Task 9  What is the password for the administrator user? Task 10 Task 11 Submit Flag those task help you to open this ctf
overview.info:
Target: 10.129.167.141
Scope: Task 1  When visiting the web service using the IP address, what is the domain that we are being redirected to? Task 2  Which scripting language is being used on the server to generate webpages? Task 3  What is the name of the URL parameter which is used to load different language versions of the webpage? Task 4  Which of the following values for the page parameter would be an example of exploiting a Local File Include (LFI) vulnerability: "french.html", "//10.10.14.6/somefile", "../../../../../../../../windows/system32/drivers/etc/hosts", "mimikatz.exe" Task 5  Which of the following values for the page parameter would be an example of exploiting a Remote File Include (RFI) vulnerability: "french.html", "//10.10.14.6/somefile", "./../../../../../../../windows/system32/drivers/etc/hosts", "mimikatz.exe" Task 6  What does NTLM stand for? Task 7  Which flag do we use in the Responder utility to specify the network interface? Task 8  There are several tools that take a NetNTLMv2 challenge/response and try millions of passwords to see if any of them generate the same response. One such tool is often referred to as john, but the full name is what?. Task 9  What is the password for the administrator user? Task 10 Task 11 Submit Flag those task help you to open this ctf
Task 1  When visiting the web service using the IP address, what is the domain that we are being redirected to? Task 2  Which scripting language is being used on the server to generate webpages? Task 3  What is the name of the URL parameter which is used to load different language versions of the webpage? Task 4  Which of the following values for the page parameter would be an example of exploiting a Local File Include (LFI) vulnerability: "french.html", "//10.10.14.6/somefile", "../../../../../../../../windows/system32/drivers/etc/hosts", "mimikatz.exe" Task 5  Which of the following values for the page parameter would be an example of exploiting a Remote File Include (RFI) vulnerability: "french.html", "//10.10.14.6/somefile", "./../../../../../../../windows/system32/drivers/etc/hosts", "mimikatz.exe" Task 6  What does NTLM stand for? Task 7  Which flag do we use in the Responder utility to specify the network interface? Task 8  There are several tools that take a NetNTLMv2 challenge/response and try millions of passwords to see if any of them generate the same response. One such tool is often referred to as john, but the full name is what?. Task 9  What is the password for the administrator user? Task 10 Task 11 Submit Flag those task help you to open this ctf

blocks.name: Service Inventory
blocks.goal: Map open services, routes, and transport exposure.

blocks.name: Exposure Posture
blocks.goal: Check for reachable database and service exposure clues on the Linux server target.

blocks.name: Service Inventory
blocks.goal: Map open services, routes, and transport exposure.
blocks.selection_rationale: Kept route_topology as it is allowed and useful for network exposure. Added a scoped curl command to check HTTP redirects for Task 1, which is non-intrusive and aligns with the target's web service focus.
blocks.summary: Service inventory block executed with mixed results. Route topology tools failed to establish connectivity or map network paths to 10.129.167.141, indicating potential network-level restrictions or host unavailability. A scoped curl command to check HTTP redirects also failed to return a response, leaving the domain redirection question unanswered.
key_findings: No network path or open ports identified to 10.129.167.141 via MTR or Nmap.
HTTP HEAD request to 10.129.167.141 timed out without response.
risk_signals: Host 10.129.167.141 appears unreachable at network and transport layers, which may indicate firewall filtering, network segmentation, or host downtime.
open_questions: Is 10.129.167.141 intentionally filtered, or is the host offline?
Are there alternative ports or protocols (e.g., HTTPS) that might bypass current restrictions?
Does the target require specific interface binding or source IP whitelisting?
artifacts: MTR and Nmap commands executed but returned no successful hops or open ports.
Curl -I command timed out without HTTP headers or redirect response.

results.summary: Network path discovery and port scanning failed; no hops, open ports, or OS guesses obtained for 10.129.167.141.
artifacts: MTR and Nmap execution logs show timeouts and no successful probes.

results.summary: Curl -I request to http://10.129.167.141 timed out without returning HTTP headers or redirect location.
artifacts: Curl command output shows 0% data received after timeout.

blocks.name: Exposure Posture
blocks.goal: Check for reachable database and service exposure clues on the Linux server target.
blocks.selection_rationale: Skipped as the target is a linux_server with a web service focus (Tasks 1-5, 11). db_enum_and_audit is not relevant here and may not be authorized for this scope. No evidence of database exposure in the tasks or targe...
blocks.summary: The 'Exposure Posture' block was skipped for the linux_server target IP_001__. The scope focuses on web service analysis (e.g., redirection, scripting language, URL parameters, and LFI/RFI vectors), with no evidence or authorization for database exposure checks.
key_findings: The 'Exposure Posture' block was skipped for the linux_server target IP_001__. The scope focuses on web service analysis (e.g., redirection, scripting language, URL parameters, and LFI/RFI vectors), with no evidence or authorization for database exposure ch...

results.summary: Block skipped due to irrelevance to the defined web service scope and lack of authorization for database enumeration.

program.name: Service Inventory
program.goal: Map open services, routes, and transport exposure.

tools.reason: Check HTTP redirect and domain response for Task 1 without fetching body content.

program.selection_rationale: Kept route_topology as it is allowed and useful for network exposure. Added a scoped curl command to check HTTP redirects for Task 1, which is non-intrusive and aligns with the target's web service focus.

program.name: Exposure Posture
program.goal: Check for reachable database and service exposure clues on the Linux server target.
program.selection_rationale: Skipped as the target is a linux_server with a web service focus (Tasks 1-5, 11). db_enum_and_audit is not relevant here and may not be authorized for this scope. No evidence of database exposure in the tasks or targe...

updates.title: Validate network reachability to 10.129.167.141 on common ports (80, 443, 8080, 8443) using alternative tools to confirm host status and...
updates.summary: The target 10.129.167.141 is unreachable on ports 80, 443, 8080, and 8443, confirmed by multiple tools and network diagnostics showing high packet loss.

updates.title: Test for HTTP/HTTPS redirection by sending a `GET` request to `http://10.129.167.141` and analyzing the `Location` header for the redirec...
updates.summary: No HTTP/HTTPS redirection was detected; the target was unreachable, returning connection errors and no Location header.

updates.title: Test reachability to 10.129.167.141 on non-standard ports (81, 3000, 8000) using `approved tooling -Pn -p 81,3000,8000 10.129.167.141`.
updates.summary: Ports 81, 3000, and 8000 on 10.129.167.141 are filtered (blocked by network controls), confirming no reachable services or vulnerability.

updates.title: Compile a list of common scripting languages (PHP, Python, Ruby, Node.js, ASP.NET) and their HTTP header signatures.
updates.summary: The finding is an informational reconnaissance task, not a vulnerability, and no security impact was identified.

updates.title: Compile a list of common scripting languages (PHP, Python, Ruby, Node.js, ASP.NET) and their HTTP header signatures.
updates.summary: The target is unreachable, preventing any HTTP header or scripting language analysis. No evidence of a vulnerability exists.

updates.title: Research common flags for specifying network interfaces in tools like `tcpdump`, `Responder`, and `approved tooling` (Task 7).
updates.summary: Network interface flags for `tcpdump` (`-i`), `tshark` (`-i`), and `Responder` (`-I`) were confirmed through documentation and help menus. No vulnerability was present.

items.name: Validate network reachability to 10.129.167.141 on common ports (80, 443, 8080, 8443) using alternative tools (e.g., `nc`, `telnet`, or `nmap -Pn`) to confirm host status and bypass potential ICMP/TCP restrictions.
Test for HTTP/HTTPS redirection by sending a `GET` request to `http://10.129.167.141` and analyzing the `Location` header for the redirected domain (Task 1).
Identify the scripting language used to generate webpages by analyzing HTTP response headers (e.g., `X-Powered-By`, `Server`) and file extensions (e.g., `.php`, `.asp`, `.jsp`) (Task 2).
Enumerate URL parameters by spidering the web service or analyzing browser/dev tools network requests to identify the parameter used for language versions (Task 3).

Test for Local File Include (LFI) vulnerability by submitting payloads like `../../../../../../../../etc/passwd` or `../../../../../../../../windows/system32/drivers/etc/hosts` via the identified language parameter (Task 4).
Test for Remote File Include (RFI) vulnerability by submitting payloads like `http://10.10.14.6/malicious.txt` or `//10.10.14.6/somefile` via the identified language parameter (Task 5).
Validate whether the server exposes NTLM (NT LAN Manager) authentication headers or responses (Task 6).
Assess network interface configuration by reviewing server responses or error messages for clues about interface binding (e.g., `eth0`, `ens33`) (Task 7).
Identify tools or services running on the server that may process NetNTLMv2 challenge/response (e.g., Samba, Active Directory) (Task 8).

Validate whether the identified language parameter is vulnerable to LFI by testing path traversal payloads (e.g., `../../../../../../../../etc/passwd`) and reviewing server responses for file content (Task 4).

Validate whether the identified language parameter is vulnerable to RFI by testing remote file inclusion payloads (e.g., `http://attacker.com/shell.txt`) and checking for execution or inclusion (Task 5).
Test for weak or default credentials on exposed services (e.g., SSH, FTP, Samba) using tools like `hydra` or `medusa` (Task 9).
Review server configuration for misconfigured file permissions or exposed sensitive files (e.g., `.git`, `.env`, `backup.zip`) (WSTG-CONF-09).
Test for HTTP method vulnerabilities (e.g., `PUT`, `DELETE`, `TRACE`) by sending requests and analyzing server responses (WSTG-CONF-06).
Validate whether the server leaks sensitive information in error messages or debug output (e.g., stack traces, database queries).
Assess whether the server is vulnerable to RFI via the identified language parameter by testing payloads like `http://attacker.com/shell.txt` (Task 5).
Test for NTLM authentication weaknesses by capturing a NetNTLMv2 challenge/response and using tools like `hashcat` or `john` to crack the password (Task 8, Task 9).
Exploit LFI vulnerability to read sensitive files (e.g., `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, or configuration files) and extract credentials or system information (Task 4).
Exploit RFI vulnerability to include a remote malicious file (e.g., PHP reverse shell) and gain remote code execution (Task 5).
Use cracked NTLM credentials to authenticate to exposed services (e.g., Samba, SSH) and escalate access (Task 9).
Leverage weak file permissions or exposed sensitive files to extract credentials or configuration details (e.g., database passwords, API keys).
Test for privilege escalation by exploiting misconfigured sudo permissions or kernel vulnerabilities (e.g., CVE-2021-4034).
